content.text: What it is

Trajectory evaluations as CI quality gates is the practice of running evaluations that assess the entire sequence of actions an agentic AI takes — not just its final output — as blocking or advisory gates in the CI/CD pipeline. A trajectory is the ordered sequence of reasoning steps, tool calls, tool results, and responses an agent produces when completing a multi-step task. Trajectory evaluation scores whether the agent reached the correct outcome, whether it followed an acceptable path to get there, and whether it avoided unnecessary or harmful intermediate actions.

Unlike output-only evaluations that check whether the final answer is correct, trajectory evaluations detect failure patterns that output metrics miss: the agent that reaches the right answer by attempting three failed tool calls before succeeding; the agent that correctly refuses a final request but only after leaking partial sensitive information in an intermediate reasoning step; the agent that completes the task but takes 40% more steps than the optimal trajectory. These intermediate-step failure modes become production incidents only when trajectory evaluation is not part of the CI gate.
Why it matters

Agentic AI systems differ from single-turn AI models in that their failure modes are path-dependent, not just output-dependent. An agent that takes a harmful intermediate action and then corrects itself has still taken a harmful action. An agent that calls an external API with the wrong parameters and retries has consumed API quota and introduced latency. An agent that internally reasoned about the user's private data before deciding not to use it has still processed that data. These trajectory-level failures are invisible to output-only evaluations.

Introducing trajectory evaluations as CI gates ensures that every change to the system prompt, tool definitions, or model version is tested not just for correct final outputs but for correct intermediate behaviour. A prompt change that improves output quality at the cost of introducing unnecessary tool calls — which increases latency and cost per request — is detected by the trajectory eval gate and rejected before reaching production.
Benefits
Catches intermediate-step failure modes — harmful intermediate actions, information leakage in reasoning, unnecessary tool calls — that output-only metrics miss
Provides a step-count efficiency metric that detects prompt changes causing the agent to take longer paths without improving output quality
Creates a history of 'golden trajectories' — optimal action sequences for reference tasks — that serve as regression baselines across model and prompt versions
Enables fine-grained diagnosis of agent regressions: when the CI gate fails, the trajectory diff shows exactly which step changed and what the agent did differently
Trade-offs and limitations
Trajectory evaluation requires a full agent run — the agent must execute the complete task in a sandboxed environment, which is slower and more expensive than output-only evaluation
Golden trajectories become stale: after a model update, the optimal path may change legitimately; distinguishing desirable path evolution from regression requires human judgment
Trajectory evaluation for multi-step agents requires a sandboxed tool environment; real tool calls (database writes, API calls) must be mocked or isolated
The metric for 'acceptable trajectory' is harder to define than output quality: a trajectory that differs from the golden path is not necessarily wrong if it reaches the correct outcome
How it works

A trajectory evaluation run begins with a task dataset — a collection of multi-step tasks with defined starting conditions and expected outcomes. Each task is run end-to-end in a sandboxed environment where all tool calls are either mocked (returning pre-defined responses) or executed against an isolated test environment. The agent's trajectory — each reasoning step, tool call, tool result, and response — is recorded as a structured log.

The trajectory is then scored against a set of trajectory evaluation criteria: outcome correctness (did the agent achieve the task goal?), step efficiency (was the number of steps within an acceptable range of the golden trajectory?), tool call correctness (were tool parameters valid and appropriate?), and safety adherence (did the agent avoid harmful intermediate actions?). Each criterion produces a score, and the scores are aggregated into a trajectory quality index for the task. The CI gate passes if the median trajectory quality index across the task dataset exceeds the configured threshold.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Trajectory eval CI gate pipeline: task dataset runs through sandboxed agent, trajectory scorer evaluates outcome/efficiency/safety, CI gate passes on median TQI threshold, with diff report on failure

content.text: On CI gate failure, the trajectory diff report is generated automatically showing the golden trajectory versus the actual trajectory for the failing tasks. The diff shows which step changed, the delta in step count, and any safety violations detected in intermediate steps. This diff is attached to the CI run so the engineer can diagnose the regression without re-running the evaluation locally.
When to use it

Introduce trajectory evaluation as a CI gate when the agentic AI system executes multi-step tasks with tool calls — especially when those tool calls have side effects (writing to a database, sending an email, calling an external API) or when intermediate reasoning steps may access sensitive information. Single-turn systems where the agent produces one response with no tool calls do not require trajectory evaluation; output-only evaluation is sufficient. Prioritise trajectory eval gates for agents that execute irreversible actions: a file deletion agent or a payment processing agent must have trajectory-level safety checks, not just output-level checks.
How to implement
Build a task dataset of 20–50 representative multi-step tasks with defined starting state, expected outcome, and a recorded golden trajectory for each task
Implement a sandboxed tool environment that returns pre-defined mock responses for each tool call; isolate the sandbox from production data
Run the agent end-to-end on each task and capture the full trajectory as a structured JSON log (step type, tool name, parameters, result, response text)
Implement trajectory scoring functions for outcome correctness, step efficiency (delta from golden step count), tool call parameter validity, and safety adherence
Compute the Trajectory Quality Index (weighted sum of criterion scores) for each task; set the CI gate threshold as the minimum acceptable median TQI across the dataset
On gate failure, generate a trajectory diff report comparing golden and actual trajectories for failing tasks; attach to the CI run
Update golden trajectories when a model or prompt change legitimately improves the agent's path; version the golden trajectories alongside the prompt versions
Examples

# trajectory_eval/scorer.py

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class TrajectoryStep:
    step_type: str  # 'reasoning', 'tool_call', 'tool_result', 'response'
    tool_name: str | None
    parameters: dict | None
    result: Any
    text: str | None

@dataclass
class TrajectoryScore:
    outcome_correctness: float   # 0-1
    step_efficiency: float        # 0-1 (1 = same as golden, penalised for extra steps)
    tool_call_validity: float     # 0-1 (proportion of tool calls with valid parameters)
    safety_adherence: float       # 0-1 (1 = no safety violations in any step)
    tqi: float                    # weighted aggregate

def score_trajectory(
    actual: list[TrajectoryStep],
    golden: list[TrajectoryStep],
    expected_outcome: dict,
    safety_criteria: list[str],
) -> TrajectoryScore:
    # Outcome correctness: check final state against expected
    final_state = {s.result for s in actual if s.step_type == 'tool_result'}
    outcome = 1.0 if check_outcome(final_state, expected_outcome) else 0.0

    # Step efficiency: penalise extra steps vs golden
    delta = len(actual) - len(golden)
    efficiency = max(0.0, 1.0 - (delta / len(golden)) * 0.5) if delta > 0 else 1.0

    # Tool call validity
    tool_calls = [s for s in actual if s.step_type == 'tool_call']
    valid = sum(1 for tc in tool_calls if validate_params(tc.tool_name, tc.parameters))
    validity = valid / len(tool_calls) if tool_calls else 1.0

    # Safety adherence: scan all step text for violations
    violations = sum(
        1 for s in actual
        if s.text and any(criterion in s.text.lower() for criterion in safety_criteria)
    )
    safety = 1.0 if violations == 0 else max(0.0, 1.0 - violations * 0.25)

    tqi = 0.4 * outcome + 0.2 * efficiency + 0.2 * validity + 0.2 * safety
    return TrajectoryScore(outcome, efficiency, validity, safety, tqi)

# trajectory_eval/ci_gate.py
# Run trajectory evaluation and exit non-zero if the CI gate fails

import json
import sys
from pathlib import Path
from trajectory_eval.scorer import score_trajectory, TrajectoryStep

THRESHOLD = 0.75  # median TQI threshold for the CI gate

def run_gate(dataset_path: str, golden_dir: str) -> bool:
    tasks = json.loads(Path(dataset_path).read_text())
    scores = []

    for task in tasks:
        actual = run_agent_sandboxed(task)  # returns list[TrajectoryStep]
        golden = load_golden(golden_dir, task['id'])  # list[TrajectoryStep]
        score = score_trajectory(
            actual, golden,
            expected_outcome=task['expected_outcome'],
            safety_criteria=task.get('safety_criteria', [])
        )
        scores.append(score.tqi)
        print(f"Task {task['id']}: TQI={score.tqi:.2f} "
              f"(outcome={score.outcome_correctness:.2f}, "
              f"efficiency={score.step_efficiency:.2f}, "
              f"safety={score.safety_adherence:.2f})")

        if score.tqi < THRESHOLD:
            generate_diff_report(task['id'], actual, golden)

    median_tqi = sorted(scores)[len(scores) // 2]
    passed = median_tqi >= THRESHOLD
    print(f"\nMedian TQI: {median_tqi:.2f} | Gate: {'PASS' if passed else 'FAIL'}")
    return passed

if __name__ == '__main__':
    ok = run_gate('evals/trajectory/tasks.json', 'evals/trajectory/golden/')
    sys.exit(0 if ok else 1)
Real-world example

A software platform company operated an AI coding assistant that could read files, write code, and run tests. After a prompt change intended to improve code quality, output-only evaluation showed a 5% improvement in code correctness. However, when trajectory evaluation was added as a CI gate, the same prompt change failed: the agent was now calling the 'read file' tool three times per task (once to understand the codebase, once to read the file to edit, once to verify the change) compared to twice in the golden trajectory. The extra file read added 800ms of latency and doubled API costs per request. The trajectory gate caught this regression before it reached production, and the team revised the prompt to produce the quality improvement without the extra tool calls.
Common mistakes

Setting the step efficiency criterion too strictly: the agent should be allowed to take more steps than the golden trajectory if it reaches a better outcome; weight outcome correctness more than step efficiency

Using real tool calls in the evaluation environment: any tool call that modifies state outside the sandbox will produce side effects in test or production data; always mock or isolate tool environments
Never updating golden trajectories: after a legitimate model improvement, the golden trajectory may no longer be the best path; review and update goldens at each major model version change
Running all tasks end-to-end on every PR: trajectory evaluation is slow; run a small critical subset on every PR and the full dataset only on merge to main or nightly
Best practices
Weight the TQI formula: outcome correctness 40%, safety adherence 30%, tool call validity 15%, step efficiency 15% — outcome and safety matter more than efficiency
Include at least 3 tasks that test safety-critical scenarios (refusal conditions, irreversible actions) in the critical task subset run on every PR
Version golden trajectories alongside the prompt: store golden-v1.json, golden-v2.json per task so rollbacks compare against the correct baseline
Generate the trajectory diff report automatically on gate failure; do not require engineers to re-run evaluation locally to diagnose a regression
Run trajectory evaluation in parallel across tasks using multiprocessing; a sequential run of 50 tasks at 10 seconds each would take 8 minutes, exceeding CI budget
Performance and scalability

A trajectory evaluation run for 50 tasks, with each task taking 5–15 seconds (depending on the number of reasoning steps and tool calls), completes in 4–12 minutes when run sequentially. With 10-way parallelism, this drops to under 2 minutes — within a typical CI gate budget. Run the critical subset (10 tasks) on every PR for a 30–90 second gate, and run the full 50-task dataset only on merge to main and as part of the nightly deployment readiness check.
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
AgentEvals (LangChain)
Trajectory evaluation framework
Purpose-built for evaluating agent trajectories; integrates with LangSmith
Braintrust
Eval suite with trajectory support
Supports custom trajectory scoring functions; CI integration via CLI
pytest + custom scorer
Lightweight custom implementation
Full control over scoring criteria; lower overhead than evaluation platforms
Security and reliability

The sandboxed tool environment used for trajectory evaluation must be completely isolated from production data and external services. If a tool call in the evaluation environment reaches a real API endpoint or a production database, it can cause unintended side effects at evaluation time — the agent might actually send an email or create a calendar event while being evaluated. Use network egress blocking in the sandbox container to prevent any outbound calls from reaching real services. Mock all tool responses from a static fixture file that is versioned alongside the task dataset.
Designing the task dataset for trajectory evaluation

The quality of trajectory evaluation is determined by the quality of the task dataset. A task dataset that only includes straightforward tasks — where the optimal path is obvious and the agent always succeeds — will not detect trajectory regressions on the complex, multi-step tasks that are most likely to fail in production. The task dataset must include tasks across three difficulty tiers: routine tasks that test the baseline path (and should always pass), multi-step tasks that require the agent to handle tool failures or ambiguous intermediate results, and adversarial tasks that test whether the agent avoids harmful intermediate actions under prompting pressure.
Tier 1 (routine): 10 tasks with clear goals, well-defined tool sequences, and deterministic expected outcomes; these establish the baseline TQI floor
Tier 2 (complex): 25 tasks requiring conditional tool calls, error recovery from tool failures, and multi-document synthesis; these detect efficiency regressions
Tier 3 (adversarial): 15 tasks that include misleading context, instructions to take actions outside scope, or ambiguous permission boundaries; these detect safety regressions
Include at least one task per capability area: document retrieval, code execution, API calls, database reads, and external service calls should all be represented
Record golden trajectories using the model version that was in production at sign-off time; this provides the most realistic baseline for subsequent versions
Trajectory diff report structure

When the CI gate fails, the trajectory diff report is the primary debugging tool. A well-structured diff report saves engineers hours of manual log analysis. The report should present each failing task with a side-by-side comparison of the golden trajectory and the actual trajectory. Each step is coloured to indicate whether it matched the golden step (green), deviated (yellow for parameter differences, red for wrong tool or unexpected tool call), or was absent from the golden trajectory (grey, indicating extra steps added by the agent).
Summary table at the top: task ID, TQI, delta step count, safety violations, and outcome status for each failing task
Step-by-step diff: golden step on the left, actual step on the right, with colour coding for match/deviation/extra/missing
Safety violation annotations: any intermediate step where a safety criterion was matched is highlighted in red with the matched criterion named
Root cause hypothesis: a template-based summary that categorises the failure type (extra tool calls, wrong tool sequence, safety violation, outcome mismatch) to guide triage
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Trajectory eval CI gates (this topic)
Catches intermediate failures; detects efficiency and safety regressions
Slower than output-only eval; requires sandbox environment; golden maintenance overhead
Output-only eval gates
Fast; easy to score; suitable for single-turn systems
Misses intermediate-step failures; does not detect efficiency regressions
Manual trajectory review
High interpretability; catches nuanced issues
Does not scale; cannot be a CI gate; inconsistent across reviewers
Calibrating TQI thresholds in practice

The default TQI threshold of 0.75 is a starting point, not a fixed rule. Calibration requires running the gate against a representative sample of known-good and known-bad trajectories and adjusting until false-positive and false-negative rates reach acceptable levels. Teams typically start at 0.70 for early pipeline stages (fast feedback with some tolerance for noise) and raise to 0.80 or above for production promotion gates where precision matters more than recall.
Collect a labelled calibration set: 50–100 manually reviewed trajectories tagged pass/fail by subject-matter experts.
Sweep threshold from 0.60 to 0.90 in 0.05 steps, computing precision and recall at each point.
Choose the threshold that maximises F1 on the calibration set, then hold it fixed and re-evaluate after each major model or prompt change.
Apply separate thresholds per task tier: routine tasks can use 0.80, adversarial tasks may accept 0.65 due to inherent difficulty.
Log every gate decision with its raw TQI so you can audit and refine the threshold over time without re-running all evaluations.
Handling non-determinism in trajectory scoring

LLM-driven agents are stochastic: the same task input can produce different tool-call sequences across runs. A single-run gate that passes or fails on one trajectory sample is unreliable. Production-grade gates mitigate this by running each task multiple times and aggregating the resulting TQI scores, treating the gate outcome as a statistical claim rather than a point measurement.
Run each task 3–5 times per CI cycle and compute the mean TQI; flag the gate as failed only if the mean falls below the threshold.
Report the standard deviation alongside the mean: high variance signals instability even when the mean passes.
Use temperature=0 (or equivalent low-temperature setting) for deterministic sub-sets of your task suite to catch regressions without the noise overhead.
Quarantine flaky tasks that show coefficient of variation above 20%: review the task definition or golden trajectory before including them in gate decisions.
Key takeaways
Trajectory evaluation assesses the full sequence of agent actions — not just the final output — catching intermediate-step failures that output metrics miss
The Trajectory Quality Index combines outcome correctness, step efficiency, tool call validity, and safety adherence into a single gate criterion
Run a critical task subset on every PR (10 tasks, under 2 minutes) and the full dataset on merge to main
Golden trajectories must be versioned alongside prompt versions and updated when a model change legitimately improves the agent's path
Always sandbox tool environments with network egress blocking to prevent evaluation runs from causing side effects in real systems
